perturbation.original_text: Developer shall be fully liable, without limitation, for money damages resulting from its improper or unauthorized use, modification, alteration, licensing or transfer of the Technology or Improvements, or resulting from its failure to provide functional and merchantable Improvements hereunder, which failure shall be deemed a material breach of this Agreement by Developer.
perturbation.changed_text: Developer shall be liable for money damages resulting from its improper or unauthorized use, modification, alteration, licensing or transfer of the Technology or Improvements, or resulting from its failure to provide functional Improvements hereunder, which failure shall be deemed a breach of this Agreement by Developer.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'without limitation' and 'merchantable', the developer's liability is reduced. Deeming the failure a 'material breach' is also removed which limits DSS's remedies. This is a material breach if the changes are not of adequate quality, or if there are significant deviations from the expectations of DSS.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Uniform Commercial Code § 2-314 Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law implies a warranty of merchantability in contracts for the sale of goods. By weakening the liability for failure to provide 'merchantable' improvements and removing 'without limitation', the contract attempts to circumvent the UCC's implied warranty, potentially limiting the remedies available to DSS if the developed software is not fit for its ordinary purpose. The original clause ensured greater protection for DSS, whereas the modified clause does not protect from the quality of work.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 38 Uniform Commercial Code ARTICLE 2 Sales PART 3 General Obligation and Construction of Contract previous SECTION 2-313 Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample up PART 3 General Obligation and Construction of Contract next SECTION 2-315 Implied Warranty: Fitness for Particular Purpose This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-12-26 2015-01-02 2015-02-20 2015-02-27 2016-05-20 2016-08-26 2016-09-16 2018-08-31 2018-10-05 2021-12-03 2022-01-28 2022-06-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 2-314 Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) CHAPTER 38, ARTICLE 2, PART 3 Section 2--314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2--316), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) in the case of fungible goods, are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) run, within the variations permitted by the agreement, of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) conform to the promises or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2--316) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade.
perturbation.justification: The removal of 'without limitation' and 'merchantable' significantly reduces the developer's liability, contradicting the implied warranty of merchantability as described in UCC 2-314. The original text provided greater protection by ensuring improvements were functional, merchantable, and the liability was without limit. The modified text now only requires 'functional' improvements, removes liability limitations, and weakens DSS's remedies. UCC 2-314 (1) states that 'Unless excluded or modified (Section 2--316), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind.' By removing the explicit requirement of 'merchantable' improvements, the contract is attempting to circumvent this implied warranty, which covers goods that are 'fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used' (UCC 2-314 (2)(c)). This omission creates a legal loophole, making it more difficult for DSS to seek remedies if the improvements are not of adequate quality, and therefore there is a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York without regard to conflict of laws principles. It is hereby irrevocably agreed that legal jurisdiction and venue for any proceeding arising out of this Agreement shall be in the state or federal courts located in the County of Monroe, State of New York, United States.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York without regard to conflict of laws principles. It is hereby agreed that legal jurisdiction and venue for any proceeding arising out of this Agreement shall be in the courts located in the State of New York, United States.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'irrevocably agreed' and removing the specification of 'state or federal courts located in the County of Monroe' this modification opens the door for the developer to claim that jurisdiction and venue are not strictly limited to Monroe County, weakening DSS's ability to control where legal proceedings take place. This modification introduces a legal risk by deviating from the original specific jurisdiction clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 503(a)
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR § 503(a) establishes rules for determining proper venue in civil actions. By removing the specification of Monroe County as the jurisdiction, the modified text contradicts the intent of the original clause to establish a specific, agreed-upon venue. This allows the developer to challenge the venue based on their own location, potentially violating the initial agreement of both parties to litigate exclusively in Monroe County, New York. The original clause offered more control to DSS for being in the right jurisdiction, and thus the modified clause introduces legal risk.

Venue based on residence. (a) Generally. Except where otherwise prescribed by law, the place of trial shall be in the county in which one of the parties resided when it was commenced; the county in which a substantial part of the events or omissions giving rise to the claim occurred; or, if none of the parties then resided in the state, in any county designated by the plaintiff. A party resident in more than one county shall be deemed a resident of each such county. (b) Executor, administrator, trustee, committee, conservator, general or testamentary guardian, or receiver. An executor, administrator, trustee, committee, conservator, general or testamentary guardian, or receiver shall be deemed a resident of the county of his appointment as well as the county in which he actually resides. (c) Corporation. A domestic corporation, or a foreign corporation authorized to transact business in the state, shall be deemed a resident of the county in which its principal office is located; except that such a corporation, if a railroad or other common carrier, shall also be deemed a resident of the county where the cause of action arose. (d) Unincorporated association, partnership, or individually-owned business. A president or treasurer of an unincorporated association, suing or being sued on behalf of the association, shall be deemed a resident of any county in which the association has its principal office, as well as the county in which he actually resides. A partnership or an individually-owned business shall be deemed a resident of any county in which it has its principal office, as well as the county in which the partner or individual owner suing or being sued actually resides. (e) Assignee. In an action for a sum of money only, brought by an assignee other than an assignee for the benefit of creditors or a holder in due course of a negotiable instrument, the assignee's residence shall
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the original agreement by removing the specification of Monroe County as the exclusive venue for legal proceedings. The original text stated that jurisdiction would 'irrevocably' be in Monroe County, while the modified text broadens the venue to any court in New York State. Scraped Law Snippet 1, NY CPLR § 503(a), discusses venue based on residence. By removing the Monroe County specification, the modified text allows the developer to potentially challenge the venue based on their residence or other factors, undermining the original intent to litigate exclusively in Monroe County, creating a contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both point to the weakening of DSS's ability to control where legal proceedings take place. Because the contract now opens the door to litigation in other counties, removing DSS's control, a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that a court of competent jurisdiction finds any provision of this Agreement to be illegal, invalid or unenforceable, it is the intention of the parties that such court shall modify such provision as necessary so that it shall be legal, valid and enforceable. The illegality, invalidity or unenforceability of any provision of this Agreement shall not affect the legality, validity or enforceability of any other provision of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that a court finds any provision of this Agreement to be illegal, invalid or unenforceable, the illegality, invalidity or unenforceability of any provision of this Agreement shall affect the legality, validity or enforceability of any other provision of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'of competent jurisdiction' and 'it is the intention of the parties that such court shall modify such provision as necessary so that it shall be legal, valid and enforceable', the scope of the clause is broadened. Furthermore, the term 'shall not affect' is changed to 'shall affect' which means that if one provision is illegal, then the whole document is also illegal, which is not what was original intended. The initial protection of DSS is removed with this omission.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York General Obligations Law § 5-321 Agreements exempting lessors from liability for negligence are void and unenforceable
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GOL § 5-321 voids agreements that exempt lessors from liability for negligence. While this agreement isn't strictly a lease, the principle applies that contracts should not shield parties from the consequences of their negligence. The original severability clause aimed to preserve the contract's validity even if some parts were deemed unenforceable. By changing it to 'shall affect', if a provision is found to be unenforceable due to negligence, the entirety of the contract may be jeopardized, thus contradicting the intent to uphold the agreement as much as possible, creating a legal risk.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-321 Agreements exempting lessors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 3 § 5-321. Agreements exempting lessors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable. Every covenant, agreement or understanding in or in connection with or collateral to any lease of real property exempting the lessor from liability for damages for injuries to person or property caused by or resulting from the negligence of the lessor, his agents, servants or employees, in the operation or maintenance of the demised premises or the real property containing the demised premises shall be deemed to be void as against public policy and wholly unenforceable.
perturbation.justification: The original contract contains a severability clause that ensures that if one part of the agreement is deemed illegal, the remaining parts remain valid and enforceable. The changed text reverses this protection by stating that the illegality of one provision 'shall affect' the legality of the other provisions. This directly contradicts the purpose of a severability clause, which is to maintain the contract's integrity even if some parts are flawed. The Law Explanation states that this creates a legal risk, since if a provision is found to be unenforceable due to negligence, the entirety of the contract may be jeopardized, thus contradicting the intent to uphold the agreement as much as possible. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically NY GOL § 5-321, voids agreements that exempt lessors from liability for negligence, establishing the principle that contracts should not shield parties from negligence consequences. The modified text weakens the contract's ability to uphold its overall agreement, if one part of it is deemed negligent.
